data.title: ADMA Biologics: Differentiated Plasma Approach With Asymmetric Upside Potential (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
ADMA Biologics, Inc. (ADMA) is a biologics company that produces immune globulin (IG) treatments. These products are helpful for treating immune deficiencies and for infectious disease indications. The company remains a small player in a large IG market, and it expects to expand its market share through its differentiated ASCENIV franchise and by growing its high-titer plasma supply network. Recently, ADMA faced scrutiny after a short-seller report challenged the quality of its reported 2025 growth. However, ADMA has rejected those claims. In my view, my initial bull thesis remains valid but at an even more compelling entry price today.

Asymmetric Upside On IG Products

ADMA Biologics, Inc., is a commercial biopharmaceutical company that develops biologics from plasma, especially immune globulin treatments for patients with immune deficiencies or at risk due to infectious diseases. ADMA was founded back in 2004 and is currently headquartered in Ramsey, New Jersey. ADMA has a manufacturing campus in Boca Raton, Florida, and its BioCenters subsidiary is headquartered in Cary, North Carolina. Additionally, ADMA’s biocenters are FDA-licensed facilities where people donate plasma. I previously covered ADMA last August, but since then, the stock has declined by 43.6%, which is why I thought it was worthwhile revisiting this name.

As a quick recap, ADMA’s portfolio includes three FDA-approved products: ASCENIV, BIVIGAM, and Nabi-HB. ASCENIV and BIVIGAM are intravenous immune globulin (IVIG). ASCENIV is indicated for Primary Immunodeficiency Disease (PIDD) in adults and adolescents aged 12 to 17. These are made using a patented donor-screening and plasma-pooling methodology, including plasma with elevated antibodies against Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV).

Having said that, BIVIGAM is more of a broad-antibody IVIG that contains antibodies that are similar to human plasma. BIVIGAM is indicated for PIDD in adults and children aged 2 and older. Moreover, Nabi-HB is a hyperimmune globulin rich in antibodies against the hepatitis B virus. Nabi-HB is made from plasma donated by people who were previously vaccinated against this disease. This compound is indicated for post-exposure protection to reduce the risk of contracting the illness.

Additionally, ADMA’s pipeline includes the candidate SG-001, which is being studied in the preclinical stages. It’s a Streptococcus pneumoniae immune globulin (IG) that contains high levels of antibodies against this pathogen, which is the cause of infections in the lung, sinus, ear, bloodstream, and meningitis, especially in vulnerable people. This IG is also made from human plasma.

With that in mind, ADMA’s biocenters collect hyperimmune and normal source plasma. The company uses its own collection infrastructure and also third-party suppliers, especially for plasma with high levels of antibodies (high-titer) against specific targets for specialty IG production. The third-party agreements secure high-titer plasma supply into the late 2030s and provide access to more than 280 third-party collection centers, involving four counterparties, including Grifols and KEDPlasma.

According to the company, the IG market is large and growing to treat Primary Immunodeficiency (PI), and it is still an unmet need even though IG products have been used for decades. Some PI patients on standard IVIG still develop problems such as respiratory tract infections, bronchiectasis, and chronic lung disease because the standard treatment does not fully solve the existing problems, so there is still space for differentiated therapies.

This global plasma fractionation market could reach around $58.2 billion by 2030, and ADMA is still a small player. There are other much larger competitors, such as Takeda (TAK), CSL (CSL), Grifols (GRFS), Octapharma, and BPL, a company part of Kedrion, which have 97% of the market. Thus, I do believe there’s ample room for future growth with ADMA’s approach to IG.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, ADMA currently trades at a $2.2 billion market cap. Their latest 10-K report shows its balance sheet holds $87.6 million in cash against $72.1 million in long-term debt. Based on those figures, I estimate ADMA’s EV at approximately $2.2 billion as well. And, their business is projected to generate $778.5 million in revenues by 2027, according to analysts.

Therefore, that would price the stock at a reasonable forward EV/S of 2.8. For comparison, ADMA’s peers’ median forward EV/S is 3.3, so the stock already looks cheap from this angle. In fact, I was bullish on ADMA back in August 2025, and the stock traded at a $4.4 billion valuation at the time. So that means the stock is now at a 50% discount since then, and I don’t see any meaningful deterioration in the business to justify such a steep pullback.

If anything, management is guiding them to further improvements on their adjusted-EBITDA margins, which showcases that their business is likely strengthening, not deteriorating, as the stock price might suggest. Moreover, assuming ADMA’s topline reaches $778.5 million as projected, it could imply they’ll be generating $459.3 million in adjusted-EBITDA next year. That also implies a cheap forward EV/EBITDA of 4.8, which is compelling in its own right. But, compared to its sector’s median forward EV/EBITDA of 11.0, it highlights just how depressed ADMA’s valuation is today.

Lastly, I think it’s worth mentioning that the company is also cash flow positive. I estimate they generated $27.8 million in positive free cash flow during 2025. Note that I got that figure by simply adding ADMA’s 2025 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. But more importantly, it corroborates that they’re self-sustainable despite having to support their ongoing revenue ramp-up. Naturally, the stock is not without its risks, and there’s a short report on ADMA that may weigh on investors’ sentiment in the near term as well. Fortunately, ADMA has provided a rebuttal to that short thesis. And I think it’s worthwhile reading both sides of the argument before initiating a bull or bear position at this point.

Aside from that, I noticed ADMA’s revenues are highly concentrated on just two customers. Almost 73% of ADMA’s consolidated revenues come from Biocare and CuraScript. This dynamic inherently exposes ADMA to substantial downside if it were to lose either of those clients. I also imagine it weakens ADMA’s negotiating power, which could be a headwind to their margins in the long run. But, since management itself is actually guiding to better margins in the coming years, I don’t think this is a major risk for now, at least.

Similarly, if ASCENIV or BIVIGAM faces regulatory setbacks or clinical concerns with patients, the stock could very well trend lower. For example, we saw that ADMA voluntarily withdrew 3 BIVIGAM lots, which showcases that faulty products are always a risk with this type of business. And if faulty products reach patients, it can always cause lawsuits, regulatory holds, loss of trust, health damages, etc. But again, the fact that ADMA caught those lots on time and proactively addressed the issue gives me confidence in their ability to execute going forward.

Conclusion: “Strong Buy” Upgrade

Overall, I was already bullish on ADMA back in 2025, mostly due to its promising growth prospects and improving margins. Since then, the numbers have supported my initial bull case, as revenue has indeed increased with margins projected to improve in the coming years. Interestingly, the stock is substantially cheaper today, so I feel the risk-reward has only improved for bulls at these levels. Therefore, I believe an upgrade to “Strong Buy” makes sense because ADMA’s depressed valuation multiples offer asymmetric upside potential. Just make sure you’re fully aware of the potential risks I previously mentioned before fully committing to a long-term bullish position.

data.title: April 2026 Monthly
data.description:
Wars rarely stay where they start. The Israeli and American military campaign against Iran - the most direct confrontation with Tehran in decades - has sent shockwaves well beyond the Middle East. The fog of war obscures the tactical picture almost daily, but the economic transmission mechanisms are clear enough, and investors who mistake this for a regional skirmish over energy do so at their peril.

One-Two Punch

The first punch is inflationary. Iran is not a bit player in global commodity markets. It sits atop some of the world's most consequential supply chains - not just oil and gas, which command the headlines, but sulfur and urea, which quietly underpin the global food system. Sulfur is a critical feedstock for phosphate fertilizers. Disrupt that supply, and you are not merely raising the price of a barrel of crude; you are raising the price of wheat in Egypt, corn in Brazil, and bread in Lagos.

Energy markets feel the shock first and most visibly, but the agricultural commodity complex is where the second-order damage accumulates, slowly and with devastating effect on the world's most food-insecure populations. Industrial commodities, like aluminum and helium (essential for semiconductor fabrication), have also been disrupted.

The second punch destroys demand. That is the cruel arithmetic of commodity shocks. The same price surge that generates windfall revenues for producers squeezes household budgets everywhere else, sapping the consumer spending that drives global growth. It boosts input costs for many manufacturers, and corporate margins compress. Capital expenditure plans go on hold.

The very inflation that appears to signal economic heat is, in this context, a leading indicator of demand destruction. Central banks find themselves in the most uncomfortable of positions: facing prices that argue for tightening while growth signals argue for caution. It is stagflation's calling card, and it arrived before most were ready to receive it.

Monetary Policy Adjustments

While market participants seem to be inclined to expect a relatively short war on Iran, the pendulum of interest rate expectations swung dramatically. There was a clear bias towards expecting the easing cycle to continue before the war began. The market was sure of Bank of England and Federal Reserve rate cuts; it was less confident but retained a bias toward the European Central Bank and the Bank of Canada cutting rates themselves.

The swaps market was more extreme, but even now it is discounting around a 65% chance of a hike at the ECB's next meeting at the end of April. Three rate hikes are fully discounted for this year, and there is about a 20% chance of a fourth.

The swaps market has a little less than a 60% chance of a Bank of England hike when they too meet on April 30. Three hikes are fully discounted for this year and a little bit more. On the eve of the war, the market had two cuts fully discounted, and a small chance of a third.

Before the war, the futures market was pricing in two rate cuts by the Federal Reserve and a 40% chance of a third. It is now discounting slightly more than a 60% chance of a hike by the end of the year.

From Trade Wars to Kinetic Wars

There is a larger context that deserves attention. The United States has now bombed at least seven countries since the start of last year. Whatever one's view of the strategic rationale in any individual case, the cumulative picture is of a rules-based international order in accelerating dissolution.

The institutions and norms that once provided a framework for managing conflict - however imperfectly - are being bypassed with increasing frequency and diminishing apology. This is not a temporary disruption. It is a structural shift in the geopolitical environment in which all economic activity takes place. Risk premiums that were compressed during decades of relative stability need to be repriced, and that repricing is neither linear nor orderly.

The truth is said to be the first casualty in a war, but it was already challenged before the first strike. Battle damage assessments are contested. Escalation ladders are unpredictable. A drone strike, a tanker seizure, a miscalculation in the Strait of Hormuz - any of these can move markets in minutes with information that takes days to verify. Volatility is not a momentary condition to be waited out; it is the operating environment, and it demands to be managed accordingly.

Trusted Advisers

Which brings us to the only actionable conclusion available in moments like this: discipline matters more, not less, when the news is loudest. Diversification, position sizing, stop-loss discipline, and a clear-eyed assessment of tail risks are not optional refinements for calm markets - they are the minimum requirements for navigating turbulent ones.

The instinct to act decisively on incomplete information is precisely what fog-of-war conditions seem to tempt. The antidote is a disciplined process, a reliable framework, and trusted advisers who have seen cycles before. In markets shaped by forces this large and this fast-moving, the quality of your decision-making process is the only edge you can reliably control.

Bannockburn World Currency Index

Bannockburn's World Currency Index, a GDP-weighted basket of the currencies of the dozen largest economies, snapped a four-month advance and posted its biggest decline (about 1.25%) since last July. The decline reflected the fact that all of the components of the index fell against the dollar. In fact, the decline brought BWCI back toward the lows for the year recorded before recovering near mid-January.

Among the G10 currencies, sterling fared the best, losing "only" about 1.6%. It may have been supported by the rate expectations adjusted in the UK, which was the most extreme. Before the war began, the market's disappointment with the UK economic data saw the two-year yield approach 3.5%, the lowest since August 2024. At its peak on March 23, it briefly traded above 4.70%. The Canadian dollar fell by about 1.8%, making it the second-best performer among the G10 components.

The Australian dollar was the worst G10 component in the index. It fell by about 3.5% in March, giving back around half of what it had gained in the first two months of the year, which had put it atop the G10. The Australian dollar was helped by the two rate hikes and hawkish tone the central bank delivered. The euro and yen declined by about 2.5-2.6%.

Among the emerging market currencies in Bannockburn's World Currency Index, the Chinese yuan's 0.70% decline was the best performer. The relative stability of the yuan and Chinese interest rates may be encouraging another look by global asset managers. The Brazilian real was in second place with a little more than a 2.2% decline. The others lost more than 4%, with the Russian ruble off around 5.5%. Although the US relaxed a restriction on Russian oil, and Moscow is charging a premium for it over Brent (before the war it was at a discount), the war with Ukraine has intensified. Ukraine reportedly struck an important refinery (Kirishi) in Leningrad as well as other Russian oil processing and industrial facilities in the Baltic region.

Bannockburn's World Currency Index reached its best level since September 2024, around 92.25 in mid-February, before falling in March. Escalation of the conflict could push the index another 0.75%-1.0% lower that would bring it to around last April's lows. A ceasefire could see the BWCI back to the mid-February high. A new high would lend credence to our medium- and longer-term bearish outlook for the dollar.

U.S. Dollar: The dollar was recovering from the sell-off it incurred when the US was pressing hard on Greenland, but the war on Iran gave it new impetus. Part of it seemed driven by position adjustment, like the unwinding of carry trades and foreign investors buying back short dollar hedges on equity investments. The US is seen as more able to deal with the energy shock than Europe, and this may have encouraged some genuine safe haven demand for dollars. Although there has been a dramatic adjustment in expectations for the Federal Reserve, it has generally been less than in many other countries. Yet, in the first instance, the surge in US rates is supportive of the dollar, even though interest rate differentials have moved against it. Each time the market grasps the faintest of hopes of a short-term war, the dollar is sold off as risk is embraced. The swaps market is discounting a much higher chance that the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and the Bank of Japan will lift rates in April than the Federal Reserve. The investigation into the Federal Reserve has not formally ended, and this is holding up the confirmation process of Powell's successor, Warsh. There is precedent for Powell, who is also a governor (until 2028), to retain the post until a successor is confirmed. Meanwhile, it is possible that the complaint against the administration's 10% tariff under Section 122 of the 52-year-old trade act is heard by the US Court of International Trade. Regardless of the outcome, the eventual decision will be appealed. The 150-day limit on Section 122 legislation, without a congressional-authorized extension, ends on July 24.

Euro: In addition to fraying the alliance some more, the US-Israel war on Iran posed a new energy shock for Europe, and the euro was sold to seven-month lows in the middle of March near $1.1400. Brent crude oil rose to a decade-high premium over the US benchmark, WTI. The euro was a little above $1.18 before the war began. The ECB stood pat in March, but the hawks insist that a hike cannot be ruled out as soon as the next meeting at the end of April. The swaps market has about a 60% chance discounted for that meeting. Before the war began, the market had around a 55% chance of a cut this year and now has three hikes fully discounted and about a 10% chance of a fourth hike. This seems excessive. The ECB's updated forecasts recognize the two-way risk. The new economic projections shaved growth this year to 0.9% (from 1.2%) and next year's growth to 1.3% (from 1.4%). The inflation outlook worsened. The updated forecast is for 2.6% CPI this year (from 1.9%) and 2.0% next year (from 1.8%). Part of the ECB's reaction function may depend on how the governments respond. ECB President Lagarde explicitly called for governments to limit energy assistance to temporary and targeted efforts.

(As of March 27, indicative closing prices, previous in parentheses)

Spot: $1.1509 ($1.1812) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: $1.1607 ($1.1900) One-month forward: $1.1525 ($1.1832) One-month implied vol: 7.8% (5.9%)

Japanese Yen: The dollar rose to almost JPY160 last month, its highest level since July 2024. The war had a more muted effect on Japanese markets than it did in Europe or the United States. The 10-year yield rose a little more than 25 bp and the two-year yield rose by around 17 basis points. Before the war began, the swaps market was pricing in about 44 bp of tightening this year and is now discounting about 56 bp. The Bank of Japan's next meeting concludes on April 28. Given the upward revision in Q4 25 GDP (1.3% at an annualized rate from 0.2%) and the strong start of this year (January industrial production rose by 4.3%, more than in 2025, and a 1.7% increase in tertiary activity, which was also more than last year), we suspect the odds of a BOJ rate hike in April are more than the 69% chance being discounted by the swaps market. The largest labor union federation secured a preliminary increase for average pay of 5.26%, topping 5% for the third consecutive year. The dollar reached JPY160.30 on March 27, the first breach of JPY160 since 2024, when it got to almost JPY162. Returning there seems a strong possibility. Bank of Japan and the Ministry of Finance officials seem more sophisticated than drawing a fixed intervention line at JPY160. It has been a broad dollar advance, and the greenback's rise against the yen has been orderly. The yen's 2.5% loss puts it near the middle of the performance of G10 currencies since the war began. It has not simply been a one-way market. One-month implied volatility is firm, mostly chopping between 9% and 10% this month, well off the January peak near 11.5%.

Spot: JPY160.31 (JPY156.05) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: JPY158.52 (JPY154.00) One-month forward: JPY159.90 (JPY155.65). One-month implied vol: 9.9% (9.2%)

British Pound: The British economy was struggling before the war began, and the fiscal headroom was limited. The economy unexpectedly stagnated in January. Industrial production contracted, and services output was flat. Before the war began, the swaps market was convinced that the Bank of England would cut rates at least twice this year, with around an 80% chance of a cut in March. The spike in energy prices caused a violent shift in the market's outlook. The BOE stood pat at last month's meeting, and some members of the Monetary Policy Committee seemed open to a rate hike. The next meeting is at the end of April, and the swaps market has about a 50% chance of a hike discounted and almost a 75% chance of at least two more hikes this year. The two-year yield jumped more than 100 bp last month. Despite a small pullback, it is still up around 95 bp. This seems to be excessive. And to be sure, sterling found little comfort from the surge in rates. After posting a February high near $1.3715, sterling overshot the $1.3250-$1.3300 target we suggested and found support closer to $1.3220. To confirm a bottom is in place, sterling needs to re-establish a foothold above the $1.3465 area, while a break of $1.3180-$1.3200 could encourage a move toward $1.3000-50.

Spot: $1.3259 ($1.3482) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: $1.3306 ($1.3600) One-month forward: $1.3260 ($1.3480) One-month implied vol: 8.4% (7.2%)

Canadian Dollar: In the generally firmer US dollar environment in March, the Canadian dollar was among the best performers in the G10. It lost about 1.65%, only bested by the British pound. We think that the conventional narrative often exaggerates the link to oil prices. In fact, the rolling 30-day correlation between changes in WTI and the US dollar's movement against the Canadian dollar has swung from inverse to slightly positive since the middle of March, for the first time since early last November. Our alternative explanation is the Canadian dollar moves in the US orbit and in a rising greenback environment does relatively better, and the opposite is also true. We note that the volatility of the exchange rate is typically the lowest among the major currencies. The swaps market had been discounting about a 45% chance of a cut by the Bank of Canada before the war began but is now pricing in two hikes fully and about a 50% chance of a third hike. While Bank of Canada Governor Macklem was adamant that the central bank would not allow prices to fuel persistent inflation, he recognized that, unlike the energy shock in 2022, the economy is soft. In February, Canada lost the most jobs in four years. The Canadian economy has been rattled by a drop in exports and business investments, spurred by the shift in US policy. The US is engaging in separate talks with Mexico and Canada over the USMCA, with Prime Minister Carney looking to diversify its trading partners. The US dollar has risen above the downtrend line drawn off the late November 2025, January 2026, and mid-March 2026 highs. The mid-January high, near CAD1.3930, may be the next target, and then the CAD1.40 area beckons.

Spot: CAD1.3893 (CAD1.3650) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: CAD1.3795 (CAD1.3700) One-month forward: CAD1.3875 (CAD1.3630) One-month implied vol: 5.2% (4.9%)

Australian Dollar: A little more than two weeks into the war on Iran, the Reserve Bank of Australia delivered its second interest rate hike of the year. A week before the hike, the Australian dollar reached almost $0.7190, its best level since mid-2022. It spent most of the month consolidating within the range seen on March 3 (about $0.6945-$0.7125) before sliding for five consecutive sessions and pushing through support near $0.6900. The break could signal another cent, or more, of losses if there is no near-term resolution of the war in the Middle East. There has been a significant shift in market expectations for the trajectory of RBA policy for the remainder of the year. Before the war began, when the overnight cash target rate was at 3.85%, the futures market expected the rate to be around 4.20% at the end of the year. The cash target rate is now 4.10%, and the pricing in the futures market sees it at 4.82% at the end of the year. As with expectations for many of the G10 central banks, this seems excessive. The central bank meets next in early May, and the futures market has almost an 80% chance of a hike. We are less sanguine given the two hikes already delivered in succession. The Reserve Bank of New Zealand meets on April 8, and the risk of a rate hike seems minimal. The divergence of monetary policy has helped lift the Australian dollar to its best level against the New Zealand dollar since 2013, reflecting around a 4.75% move this year. A downside correction appears to have begun, which adds an additional drag on the Australian dollar.

Spot: $0.6874 ($0.7118) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: $0.6950 ($0.7000) One-month forward: $0.6875 ($0.7120) One-month implied vol: 11.8% (9.3%).

Mexican Peso: The Mexican peso had its worst month since August 2024, when it fell almost 6%. It depreciated by about 4.5% in March. Its decline was driven by several considerations. Like other high-yielding emerging market currencies, it had benefitted from carry trades. The dollar was used to fund purchases of Mexican bonds, and when the bonds were liquidated, the short dollar was covered. Another consideration was the broad risk-off activity, and this also weighed on the Mexican peso. The inverse 30-day correlation between changes in the S&P 500 and the dollar against the peso reached the most extreme since mid-2020 (-0.80 in mid-March). The central bank surprised many with a rate cut in late March to bring the policy rate to 6.75%, despite inflation pushing above the top end of the 2%-4% target. The central bank also signaled it may cut rates again. We expect that the end of hostilities will see the peso recover quickly and remain more bullish than the market, where the median in Bloomberg's survey sees the dollar around MXN17.88 at the end of the year. That said, we recognize that the USMCA talks are a wild card even though the mercurial US president negotiated what he said was the best trade agreement ever.

Spot: MXN18.1188 (MXN17.2270) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: MXN17.9743 (MXN17.60) One-month forward: MXN18.1655 (MXN17.2765) One-month implied vol: 13.9 (9.2%)

Chinese Yuan: China seems like one of the few beneficiaries of the US-Israel war on Iran. The disruption in the energy market may boost the demand for solar panels and electric vehicles, which China dominates. While China bullies its neighbors, a tragedy of small countries next to large ones, it has shown restraint compared with Russia and the United States. Ships carrying Iranian oil to it have not been molested in the Strait of Hormuz. Reportedly, Tehran is encouraging others to pay for oil in yuan for safe passage. The stability of its onshore bond market has drawn renewed interest from some global asset managers. Beijing is lifting all tariffs on more than 50 African countries, which offers a stark contrast with the US's broad tariff regime. Nor has Beijing used the broad gains of the greenback since the start of the war to depreciate the yuan. At the end of the third week of the war, the PBOC set the yuan's reference rate against the dollar at its strongest level since April 2023. Still, the rise in oil and food prices may help offset the disinflationary forces in China; it will do nothing to stimulate demand. The offshore yuan is consolidating in a roughly CNH6.86-CNH6.95 range. The dollar bottomed against the onshore yuan near CNY6.8315 in late February. We suspect it can fall to CNY6.70-CNY6.75 later this year, which is somewhat more bullish than the consensus. However, first, the PBOC will not ignore the dollar's broad strength amid the hostilities with Iran. Instead, it may acquiesce to the greenback's strength and allow it to recover back into the CNY6.9250-CNY6.9500 area.

Spot: CNY6.9112 (CNY6.8620) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: CNY6.9067 (CNY6.9250) One-month forward: CNY6.9145 (CNY6.8830) One-month implied vol: 3.7% (3.4%)

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Unity: Upgrade To Strong Buy On Excellent Preliminary Q1 Results
data.description:
I last covered Unity Software (NYSE:U) in October, moving from a prior "strong buy" back to a "buy" rating.

At the time, I wrote that: "I'm still bullish on Unity, but the higher valuation and underwhelming Q2 results reduce my enthusiasm a bit." With the benefit of hindsight, I should have sold my position entirely at that point. Because, in this market, any software companies that deliver less than exemplary results have been decimated.

While Unity has continued to show operational improvement, it hasn't been enough to offset the terrible sentiment that has afflicted the overall software industry. As a result, Unity has given back its gains from the past year and finds itself once again near its multi-year lows. This improved a bit with Friday's 13% rally, but shares are still at the lower end of the trading range:

The stock appeared to be breaking out to new highs as recently as December. After getting cut in half year-to-date, where does that leave the investment case for Unity Software going forward?

An Excellent Earnings Pre-Announcement

Unity Software appeared to be on course toward another dour week, with shares down about 10% heading into Friday's session. Understandable, given the difficult backdrop the market has been facing with the Iran War concerns along with the sell-off we've been seeing in many leading software and technology companies. However, Unity was able to snap its losing streak on Friday after pre-announcing its Q1 results:

The following table with Unity's preliminary Q1 numbers should give a sense of why sentiment turned on Friday:

As this shows, Unity now anticipates announcing roughly $506 million of revenues, which is substantially above its prior $485 million mid-point guide. This preliminary figure would represent 17% year-over-year growth.

It gets better when you dig into the details. Look at the adjusted EBITDA line, for example. The company sees a full $25 million jump in its adjusted EBITDA versus prior guidance. That will amount to a roughly 58% rise in the firm's adjusted EBITDA year-over-year. That's a huge amount of operating leverage compared to the rise in revenues.

And on revenues, Unity has been shuttering a lot of non-core business lines amid its huge headcount reductions. The company has refocused its efforts on the game engine and the associated advertising and monetization business. That has created some complexity in analyzing Unity's overall results, as the firm's strategic business has been growing significantly faster than the overall top-line results.

That really came into full focus with these preliminary Q1 results. Unity generated a roughly 48% rise in its strategic Grow (advertising) revenues and a 14% rise in its Create (game engine) revenues for Q1 versus the prior year. Overall, that equates to a 34% y-o-y increase in strategic revenues. There's been a lot of concern in the analyst community over whether Unity could get back to growth after the downturn in the gaming industry coming out of the pandemic. I think, especially with the Q1 results, it's becoming increasingly difficult to deny Unity's operational momentum in both the game engine and the ads business.

The 14% rise in strategic Create revenues is of particular interest, as analysts have heavily criticized Unity's monetization efforts in this field and predicted widespread developer defections to alternate game engines. Unity's results over the past year, and in particular with these Q1 results, seem to disprove that bearish contention.

Here is the current Unity earnings and revenues analyst consensus:

On an adjusted earnings basis, Unity is strongly profitable and trading for less than 20x earnings, with that moving to less than 12.5x in FY'28. However, Unity has a large amount of share-based compensation, which has previously kept the company from achieving consistent GAAP profitability. And now, with the firm's huge rounds of layoffs, it has had restructuring charges that further hit GAAP earnings.

Overall, Unity has let go of more than a third of its workforce in recent years. That should greatly rein in SBC going forward, and the restructuring charges should abate now that Unity has already greatly shrunk its workforce and refocused on core operations. All this to say that it's fine to take the adjusted EPS figures with a big grain of salt, but also that Unity should start generating solid GAAP earnings going forward as well.

As for revenues, analysts have Unity growing in the low teens over the next three years. Those estimates may be too low. I'd note that analysts were nearly evenly split in terms of whether they were raising or lowering their numbers heading into the Q1 print:

Given this uncertainty around whether Unity's turnaround was working or not, I think a fair chunk of the analyst community probably got caught offside with Unity's robust Q1 numbers and will need to raise estimates going forward. This could help sustain the rally in Unity stock that began on Friday.

And, in the bigger picture, recall that Unity's strategic revenues are growing at 34% year-over-year. The strategic revenues, meanwhile, are becoming a much larger chunk of the firm's overall revenues. So, it's possible that the roughly 14% forward annualized revenue growth rate that analysts have been modeling could be significantly too low. If we suppose that Unity can actually grow revenue at a closer to 18-20% rate, that could support a significant multiple expansion for Unity shares.

Epic's Big Layoff

There was one other key headline this past week for Unity. That would be that its key rival in the game engine space, Epic, announced layoffs of about 1,000 employees along with at least $500 million in other cost cuts. Epic is a diversified business which operates video games such as Fortnite, the Epic Game Store, and the Unreal game engine. This has produced synergies for Epic over the years, such as building hit games like Fortnite within the Unreal Engine.

Epic states that its layoffs are necessary due to a drop in player engagement with Fortnite. It's rough out there in the gaming industry right now, and apparently Epic hasn't been able to avoid the broader negative trends. Epic recently tried to increase monetization within Fortnite but drew a great deal of criticism from their player community.

Some analysts had suggested that Unreal, due to being operated by privately held Epic, didn't face the same budgetary constraints that Unity dealt with as a public company accountable to shareholders. However, it seems that even Epic eventually had to give in to the broader gaming industry downturn and greatly reduce headcount.

From the reporting I've seen, it's not clear how much of the Epic layoffs will hit the Unreal Engine part of the business, as opposed to Fortnite, the game store, or other operations. That said, financial downsizing at Epic should be a positive for Unity in a largely duopoly market between the two, all else held equal.

As I've explained in my prior Unity articles, I believe we're in a long-term shift from game studios using their own in-house engines to outsourcing it. And, for higher-quality games, Unreal and Unity are the only real options in town; alternatives like Godot and GameMaker simply aren't in the same league of performance. To the extent that Unreal may be hampered by fiscal cutbacks, that gives Unity more room to take share. And, judging by the reported 13% growth in strategic revenues at the Create division in Q1, Unity's game engine division is indeed back on track.

Unity Stock Verdict

Let's be frank: This is a dismal operating environment for software companies. The fact that Unity stock, overall, was up only modestly last week despite the tremendous preliminary Q1 results speaks to that.

I believe that Unity is largely immune from AI-disruption risk over the next five years. Making a high-budget video game is really hard; it's orders of magnitude more complicated than vibe-coding a SaaS dashboard replacement. The game engine is a foundational tool of the game creation process, and I see no sign of anything coming along to uproot that over the intermediate term.

And, on the ad side of the business, Unity appears to be an outright AI winner. Its AI-enhanced Vector ad platform is driving solid growth in the business, as the preliminary Q1 results demonstrate. It's possible that AI will become a net negative for Unity at some point in the more distant future, but up until now anyway, Unity appears to be managing the landscape well.

Eventually, the market will start sorting out the actual AI winners in the software space from the firms that are being actively disrupted today. I can't guarantee you that this winnowing process will start over the next quarter or two, however. It's quite possible that if software stocks keep tanking, Unity stock will make lower lows even as the operating business is picking up steam.

I want to be clear that I'm not calling for a precise bottom for software stocks. I wouldn't be surprised if the sector keeps dropping, and I am prepared to average into positions, including Unity, in the coming months as necessary. That said, I believe Unity will significantly outperform the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) in coming months and years and that the starting valuation for Unity stock is attractive at this price. Thus, I'm comfortable upgrading Unity shares to a strong buy today.

data.title: BST: Stuck Between QQQ And SOXX, Delivering Neither
data.description:
The BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (BST) is a hold in my view - more so in a regime of flat markets that I expect for the next few quarters. BST shows some structural drawbacks that require specific market conditions to work. Over the past 5 years, performance (in terms of total returns) has not been great. The gap is large enough to make other alternatives a better buy both from an income and total return perspective.

Portfolio Structure and Income

The portfolio emphasis on semis and equipment is evident with around 25% allocation to the theme in the top 4 holdings themselves. The overall semis exposure is ~38%. That places BST somewhere leaning more toward QQQ (~15-20% semis exposure) than SOXX (full semis exposure) - but both remain relevant benchmarks. In terms of mega cap platforms, Apple, Microsoft, and Alphabet together account for ~14% of the BST portfolio (within the top 10 holdings alone). QQQ appears to be a little more heavily tilted to big tech, even within the top 10 holdings alone.

The key differentiator for BST is the exposure to a private AI layer. We have Databricks, Anthropic, and Anduril, together accounting for ~13%, and this is just the data we have in the top 10 holdings. That differentiator is what could tilt a buy in BST in itself if we are in a regime where the AI trade is going strong. But on the other hand, it introduces valuation opacity and long-duration growth risks that may not do well in adverse conditions. Overall, from the portfolio construction, BST is around 55-60% QQQ, 25-30% SOXX and 10-20% private venture layer, as far as I see it (quant style attribution to broadly help map the positioning mentally).

The strategy used to implement a covered call writing mechanism in the past. I found a dated semiannual report (mid-2024) that mentions around 32% covered call writing activity. This was mentioned in mid-2025 as well. However, the 2025 annual report now confirms that option writing is no longer part of the strategy. However, the yield continues to remain significant despite no other source of funding beyond capital appreciation (assuming dividends do not account for the 5-10% yields we see in BST).

I found the 2024 distribution attribution for payouts, and that specifies $0.119 monthly as a non-taxable return of capital (ROC). That accounts for ~45% of the total monthly payouts of $0.25. Since, option writing activity has been stopped in 2025, I expect that ROC component to go up. That seems tax-friendly because ROC is not immediately taxed and reduces cost basis. However, that does not offset underperformance or ensure lower capital gains eventually. The ROC also signals insufficient earnings to support distribution - something that only becomes more of a factor now, with no option layer to fund some income.

BST in the Current Regime

My outlook for the next few quarters is a continuation of what we are seeing already. That is, a regime where multiple expansion becomes a limited possibility. AI tailwinds are still intact, but earnings will likely grow into valuations rather than another leg of rerating pulling share prices up. This impacts the platform players and hyperscalers more than the AI infrastructure layer because they are the immediate beneficiaries of a historical surge in capex trends. While BST's heavier semis exposure keeps a floor intact at the moment, the hyperscaler components offset that to an extent as markets are uncomfortable underwriting long duration risks and scrutinizing capex ROIs and timelines at high valuations.

In the net flat outlook ahead, the core portfolio should remain supported, but that is until the semis play holds up. Within the semis space too, there are concerns around front-loading demand today and the uncertainties of cyclical risks at play if capex spending slows down ahead. The private venture exposure is also more suited for 2024-25 like markets where AI trades were being rewarded. In the current setup, that advantage does not show up.

The only layer that may have helped BST's total return cause in these conditions is the option layer that could have monetized volatility and added income even as the markets stayed under pressure. But BST has stopped writing covered calls now. That keeps upside capture open, but is not the need of the hour. All in all, since the BST strategy now completely relies on the capital appreciation in the underlying holdings, expect some NAV decay as payouts are funded through ROC and the underlying portfolio stays flat to slightly negative under the combined effects of a resilient but flat semis segment and under pressure hyperscaler segment.

Difficult to See BST as a Core Allocation

The fact that BST is now trading at ~9% discount to NAV supports its thesis, but valuation opacity of private ventures limits how much confidence can be placed in that discount as a true margin of safety. The thesis so far shows BST's relatively underwhelming positioning for the current regime. A look at the total return metrics over the past 5 years seals the Hold rating.

In a regime (2021-26) where SOXX captured the full capex cycle and QQQ captured platform monetization narratives, BST's total returns are in single digits cumulatively. In other words, for a portfolio that is placed between SOXX and QQQ, the performance is not close to either. The private venture exposure could be one of the reasons, and it could see explosive growth at some stage to compensate, but that's not now and has not been seen in the past (one of the sharpest bull rallies on the AI theme). Another reason that could explain this underperformance is the presence of an option layer. That at anywhere around the ~32% levels of overwriting reported, we are only talking about a partial cap at best - not sufficient to account for a large part of the overall underperformance per se (and less relevant now because upside capture is not a mandate I am chasing today).

For total return investors, unless there is a strong reason to prefer exposure to private ventures, any of the tech ETFs ranging from SOXX to QQQ have done well and provide strong alternatives to BST. For income seekers, we have a bunch of alternatives that ride the benchmark indices and use a covered call approach to fund yields partially - QYLD, JEPQ and QQQI (for QQQ underlying), SPYI and XYLD (for S&P 500). I expect these alternatives to show greater upside capture, and continue to generate income that can compete with BST, especially in flat markets.

data.title: 10 Best CEFs This Month: Average Yield Of 10% (March 2026)
data.description:
Introduction:

Since Nov. 2025, the market has been mostly trading in a tight range between S&P 500 (SPX) levels 6600 and 6950. However, everything changed recently with the start of the full-blown Iran war. This has moved the market closer to SPX6500 rather than 7000. The prices of oil and gas have shot up and have been very volatile. Needless to say, there is a lot of uncertainty in the market, despite some indications of negotiations between the U.S. and Iran. It is too early to say how long the hostilities will last. The longer it drags, the more pain is expected for the energy prices, inflation, and the world economy. So, right now, nothing else seems to matter to the stock market. Higher energy prices will also likely tie the hands of the Fed, and any further interest-rate cuts may be off the table for now. All other worries of the market have been put on the back burner for now, but surely they will come back as soon as the dust from the war settles.

You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

All that being said, it is always hard to find anything that has both the quality and the low valuation, so buying the best of the income investments regularly in small quantities is the key. As always, investors have to tread carefully amid all the uncertainties.

Why CEFs?

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal level of yield of less than 1.10%. Income investors and retirees can't survive on that level of income unless they have a very large pool of capital. Also, the method of raising income by selling shares is generally very stressful and full of pitfalls, and that's why it is not recommended. Alternatively, one can find solid blue-chip individual dividend stocks that pay much higher dividends than the S&P 500. They can also provide market-matching growth on a longer-term basis. To identify such stocks, we also publish a monthly article on the '5 Best DGI stocks'. You can find the most recent one here.

However, with CEFs, we can take the income to the next level. That said, they generally come with higher risks (discussed later), so we believe that an allocation of 20% to 30% to a diversified CEF portfolio should be good enough for most income investors. In summary, these investments should be used primarily for income, not growth.

We publish this monthly article on CEFs (Closed-end funds) to provide our readers with a select list of 10 CEFs that, as a group, provide wide diversification in terms of underlying assets, solid long-term performance at least on a relative basis, and are trading at decent valuations. Above all, the income goal for the group is to provide a yield of at least 8% or higher.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of 10.58% (as of 03/20/2026). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 17.3%, 9.7%, and 10.9% (annualized) in the last three, five years, and 10 years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is attractive, at -10.8%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Why Is It Important To Have A Diversified CEF Portfolio?

We believe a well-diversified CEF portfolio should contain at least 10 CEFs, preferably from different asset classes. It's also advisable to build the portfolio over a period rather than invest in one lump sum. If you were to invest in one CEF every month for a year, you would have a well-diversified CEF portfolio by the year's end. What we provide here every month is a list of ten probable candidates for further research. A CEF portfolio can be an important component of the overall portfolio strategy. One should preferably have a DGI portfolio as the foundation, and the CEF portfolio could be used to boost the income level to the desired level. How much should one allocate to CEFs? Each investor needs to answer this question himself/herself based on their personal situation and factors like the size of the portfolio, income needs, risk appetite, or risk tolerance.

Criteria to Shortlist:

Criteria

Brings down the number of funds to...

Reason for the Criteria

Baseline expense < 2.5% and Avg. Daily Volume > 10,000

Approx. 435 Funds

We do not want funds that charge excessive fees. Also, we want funds that have fair liquidity.

Market capitalization> 100 Million

Approx. 400 Funds

We do not want funds that are too small.

Track record/ History longer than five years (inception date 2016 or earlier)

Approx. 375 Funds

We want funds that have a reasonably long track record.

Discount/Premium < +7%

Approx. 350 Funds

We do not want to pay too high a premium; we want bigger discounts.

Distribution (dividend) Rate > 5%

Approx. 250-290 Funds

The current distribution (income) is reasonably high.

5-Year Annualized Return on NAV > 0% AND/ OR

3-Year Annualized Return on NAV >0%

Approx. 200-250 Funds

We want funds with a reasonably good past track record in maintaining their NAVs.

After we applied the above criteria this month, we are generally left with around 250 funds (plus or minus 5%) on our list. From here on, we apply our other criteria to narrow the list.

Note: All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article is sourced from Cefconnect.com, Cefa.com, and Morningstar.com.

Narrowing Down To 50 Funds:

To narrow down the number of funds to a more manageable count, we will shortlist ten funds based on each of the following criteria. Thereafter, we will apply certain qualitative criteria to each fund and rank them to select the top ten.

At this stage, we also eliminate certain funds with substantially negative NAV returns for both three-year and five-year periods.

Seven Broad Criteria:

  • Excess discount/premium (explained below).
  • Distribution rate.
  • Return on NAV, last three years (medium-term).
  • Return on NAV, last five years (long term).
  • Coverage ratio.
  • Excess return over distributions.
  • The total weight (calculated up to this point).

Excess Discount/Premium

We certainly like funds that are offering large discounts (not premiums) to their NAVs. But what's important is to look at the "excess discount/premium" and not the absolute value. We want to see the discount (or premium) relative to their past record, say, a 52-week average.

Excess Discount/Premium = Current Discount/Premium (Minus) 52-Wk Avg. Discount/ Premium.

For more explanation of the excess discount and z-scores, please refer to our blog post (link provided at the beginning of the article).

We sort our list (of approx. 250 funds) on the "excess discount/premium" in descending order. For this criterion, the lower the value, the better it is. So, we select the top 10 funds (with the most negative values) from this sorted list.

(All data as of 03/20/2026)

Table 1:

High Current Distribution Rate

After all, most investors invest in CEF funds for their juicy distributions. Even though we should not chase yield, at this stage, we want to give them a fair representation in our list and subject them to a more rigorous criterion later on. We sorted our list on the current distribution rate (descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds from this sorted list.

Table 2:

Medium-Term Return on NAV (last three years)

We then sort our list on a three-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and select the top 10 funds. For this reason, we make a shortlist based on the total score and keep only a few of them on this list.

Table 3:

Five-Year Annualized Return on NAV

We then sorted our list on the five-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 4:

Coverage Ratio (Distributions Vs. Earnings)

The coverage ratio is derived by dividing the earnings per share by the distribution amount for a specific period. Please note that in some cases, the coverage ratio may be a bit inaccurate since the "earnings per share" may be three to six months old. But in most cases, it's fairly accurate. Furthermore, the coverage ratio is not critical for pure equity funds as they generate very little dividend income, and they cover most of their distributions from capital gains. However, we sorted our list by coverage ratio and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 5:

Excess Return Over Distribution

If a fund provides a very high distribution but low returns over 3, 5, or 10-year periods, it does not help the long-term interests of the investor. In simple terms, the fund is just overpaying the distributions and hurting the NAV in the long term. That is why we calculate a factor we like to call "excess return." This excess return is what is provided by the fund over and above the distribution rate over a specified time period. We calculate it by subtracting the distribution rate from the average of the three-year and five-year NAV returns. In this list, we included the top 10 funds.

Table 6:

Total Weight (Quality Score) Calculated Up to This Point

Note: The Total Weight calculation is not fully completed at this point since we have not considered the 10-year NAV return. However, we select the top 15 names based on current calculations.

Table 7:

From the selections so far, we have 75 funds in total (tables 1-7).

We will need to see if there are any duplicates among them. In our current list of 75 funds, there were 29 duplicates, meaning there are funds that appeared more than once. The following names appear twice (or more):

Appeared twice: AIO, BANX, BGR, BRW, DPG, EIC, EMF, EOS, JOF, KYN, PEO, TEI, and UTF (13 duplicates).

Appeared three times: CSQ, GAM, GNT, JRI, PDX, and TWN (12 duplicates).

Appeared four times: None.

Appeared five times: GGN (4 duplicates).

Once we remove 30 duplicate rows, we are left with 46 (75- 29) funds.

Note: It may be worthwhile to mention here that just because a fund has appeared multiple times does not necessarily make it an attractive candidate. Sometimes, a fund may appear multiple times simply for the wrong reasons, like a high current discount, a high excess discount, or a very high distribution rate that may not be sustainable. However, during the second filtering stage, it may not score well on the overall quality score due to other factors, like a poor track record. That said, if a fund has appeared four times or more, it may be worth a second look.

Narrowing Down to Just 10 Funds:

In our list of funds, we may already have some of the best probable candidates. However, so far, they have been selected based on one (or two) criteria that each may be good at. That's not nearly enough. So, we will apply a combination of criteria by applying weights to eight factors to calculate the total quality score and filter out the best ones.

We will apply weights to each of the eight criteria:

Criteria

Max. Weight

Comment

Baseline expense

5.0

Current distribution rate

7.5

Excess discount/premium

5.0

3-YR NAV return

5.0

5-YR NAV return

5.0

10-YR NAV return or 10-YR Total return

5.0

If less than ten years of history, an average of three and five-year returns is taken.

Excess NAV return over distribution rate

5.0

Adjusted Coverage Ratio

7.5

This factor is adjusted for certain factors. The adjustment is performed based on the type of fund, such as equity and sector funds.

Once we have calculated the weights, we combine them to calculate the "Total Combined Weight," also called the "Quality Score."

The sorted list (spreadsheet) of 46 funds on the "combined total weight or quality score" is attached here.

File-for-export_-_10_Best_CEFs_-_Mar_2026.xlsx

10-Positions Portfolio of The Month:

Before we get to the final ten, we will narrow down our list to 20 names. So, we sorted our table based on the sector and then the quality score. Furthermore, in this round of selections, we mostly pick one or two top funds from each sector (based on quality score). There is a bit of subjective judgment, as we tend to prioritize funds that pay regular and consistent distributions on a monthly or quarterly basis. We also weed out any fund that may have a very poor track record in terms of NAV performance. Funds that may have inconsistent dividends (even if they are high) generally do not make it to our top list. Furthermore, be aware that many times, single-country funds score high in our rankings, but most of them pay variable dividends on an annual or semi-annual basis. In addition, being single-country funds, they can be inherently riskier since their future returns are tied to just one country, be it economic, regulatory, or geopolitical factors. For these reasons, they generally do not make it to our top 10 list, but they do appear on our list of 20. Individual investors could consider them based on their goals.

Here's the list of the top 20 selections (from different asset classes) in the order of sector:

Table 8:

10-Best-CEFs of the Month

In the end, if we only want to select ten funds, we will need to apply our subjective judgment to some degree. While we narrow down this list to ten names, we would be mindful to keep the list as diversified as possible in terms of sectors and asset classes. Since this step is somewhat subjective, the choice would differ from person to person. Nonetheless, here are the selections for this month, based on our perspective (in order of Quality Score):

Here's the list of the top 10 selections this month (in order of sector):

(EOS), (JRI), (GHY), (EIC), (BANX), (HQL), (UTF), (AIO), (CSQ), and (GAM).

Table-9:

Some information about the selections:

We will only briefly describe the selected funds. Since there are 10 of them, we will not be providing a very detailed analysis of them.

Here are some details on many of our selections:

(GHY) PGIM Global High Yield Fund:

This is mostly a bond fund invested in high-yield fixed income securities and instruments of companies and governments all over the world, including some emerging markets. As the name indicates, it is invested globally with nearly 55% outside the U.S. The focus of this fund is on high income and the current distribution is close to 11%. We think it is overpaying somewhat as it covers only 70% to 75% of the distributions by investment income. Since there is no equity exposure, fund’s total returns are likely to be much different than the equity markets and likely to be lower in the longer term. However, it will diversify the portfolio by providing income stability. Only income focused investors should consider this fund.

(BANX) ArrowMark Financial Corp:

The fund’s primary objectives are to provide a high and stable income to its investors, while maintaining a stable NAV. The fund invests in subordinated debt, convertible securities, regulatory capital relief securities, preferred equity, and to a lesser extent, common equity.

The fund currently offers a reasonably high yield of 9.4%, which is fully covered and supported by the NII (net investment income). It is also trading at a very attractive discount of -11%. It offers exposure to floating rate instruments that have benefited from the rise in interest rates in recent years; however, that may appear to be a negative right now. But rates may not decline any further in a hurry, and certainly we are not going to see zero interest rates any time soon. Moreover, the management is quick to adapt to changing conditions and ensure that the distribution is fully covered and the NAV is maintained.

(CSQCSQ) Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund:

This fund is a hybrid fund and provides exposure to many different asset classes including equities, convertible securities, and high-yield corporate bonds. Nearly 2/3rd of CSQ's capital is invested in equities, and the rest is in convertibles, bonds, and preferred securities. Also, the equity is a bit concentrated in tech and other growth-oriented stocks, but it does represent other sectors reasonably well. With 1/3rd of the portfolio in fixed income, we would expect CSQ to underperform the S&P500, but over the longer term, it has achieved similar performance by using significant leverage of roughly 30%. However, the leverage can play on both sides, and generally increases the volatility and drawdowns during big corrections. It recently increased the distribution amount (in Jan 2026), and the current yield is quite decent at close to 8.5%.

(EOS) Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income II:

We like to have one fund in our selection that is based on covered calls. Eaton Vance fund, EOS often makes to our list as it is one of the best funds in its category. Its high exposure to the technology sector has definitely helped the fund to shine in the last 10 years. It currently holds only about 50 companies and overwrites (sells options) on roughly 50% of its portfolio to generate income and reduce volatility. The distribution yield is attractive at 9%, and currently offers a decent discount of -9.5%. The fund uses no leverage, and its expenses are comparable to those of its competitors. The past performance record is solid, and it has returned 22%, 10%, and 13% (annualized) over the last 3, 5, and 10-year periods.

(JRI) Nuveen Real Asset Income & Growth Fund

The fund JRI is a global fund offered by the Nuveen group and invests in companies that usually deal in real assets. In the context of real assets, it does not mean real-estate as such but a broad based set of companies that are engaged in owning, developing, or operating infrastructure projects or facilities, including some REITs (Real Estate Investment trusts). The fund’s investments span from equity stocks, preferred instruments, and debt securities. Nearly 50-55% of the assets are invested in the U.S.-based companies. The fund uses a significant amount of leverage (roughly 30%) and is very well diversified with nearly 450 holdings. The investments are roughly 45%:55% in real estate vs infrastructure. Also, it is invested 52% in equities, 28% in preferreds, and roughly 20% in debt securities.

The fund announced a rights offering recently, and that could be one reason that the discount has widened to -9%. The yield is attractive at 13% plus.

(AIO) Virtus AI & Tech Opp Fund:

This is a fund focused on AI (Artificial Intelligence) and technology companies. But it comes with a unique flavor of combining both the equity and convertible securities, as well as some bonds, based on market conditions. The fund also uses modest leverage of 12% -15% and has performed very well in the last three years. The current distribution yield is nice at 8% plus and is currently trading at an attractive discount of roughly -12%. The fund’s market price has come under pressure recently, and the discount has widened somewhat.

(EIC) Eagle Point Income Co:

EIC is probably the riskiest fund in this selection of 10 funds. The fund uses high leverage of 34% in the form of preferred stock. The fund primarily invests in junior debt tranches of CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations). In addition, it may invest up to 35% of total assets in CLO equity securities and related securities.

Due to the decline in interest rates, similar to its peers, the fund has been reducing its distribution amount. The monthly distribution has come down from $0.20 a share to $0.10, a 50% reduction. This has caused the price to fall during most of 2025, and that has continued in 2026, but the pace has slowed down. So, it has been brutal for someone who acquired the shares prior to 2024. However, the yield is still very attractive for the new buyers at nearly 14%, in spite of the lower distribution amount. The discount has widened to very high levels (in excess of 20%). On the positive side, with reduced distribution, the current distribution is fully covered by the NII (net investment income). We think that after significant correction, the price and yield are attractive.

(UTF) Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund:

There are two funds in the utility and infrastructure space that remain most popular and obviously for a reason. These two funds are UTF and Reaves Utility Income fund (UTG). However, UTF appears to offer better value currently compared to UTG, even though both have appreciated quite a bit in the last 12 months.

The fund invests in infrastructure companies, which consist of utilities, pipelines, toll roads, airports, railroads, ports, and telecommunications companies. The fund offers a close to 7% plus yield and a discount in excess of -8%. The fund offers a very reliable distribution payout as it has paid consistent and growing distributions since its inception.

(HQL) abrdn Life Sciences Investors

Most of the healthcare funds were in dumps from 2022-2024. However, the sector started recovery in the last 12-15 months. Same is true for our selection, HQL. It gained roughly 23% in the previous 12 months, excluding nearly 13% dividends.

The fund recently increased the distribution amounts, and the distribution yield is very attractive at 13.8%. It also offers a discount of close to -9%.

The fund uses almost zero leverage and invests in well-known names from the biotechnology sector. This fund has managed to pay a consistent distribution, but mostly from the capital gains and, to some extent, ROC (return of capital).

Some of the top holdings are Amgen (AMGN), Regeneron (REGN), Insmed Inc (INSM), UniQure NV (QURE), Vaxcyte (PCVX), and Vertex (VRTX).

(GAM) General American Investors:

Please note, income investors who like to have regular quarterly (or monthly) distributions should look elsewhere, such as Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX). That said, GAM is our selection for the core U.S. equity fund for this month. It should be noted that the fund pays annually in the month of December, and the payment is variable from one year to another. Based on the distribution paid in December 2025, the yield is quite attractive at close to 11%. The current discount is just under -10%.

The primary investment objective of the fund is to seek capital appreciation, with income only a secondary objective. The fund invests in equity securities with growth potential at reasonable valuations. The fund also uses a modest leverage of roughly 10% in the form of preferred stock.

CEF-Specific Investment Risks:

It goes without saying that CEFs generally have some additional risks. This section is specifically relevant for investors who are new to CEF investing, but in general, all CEF investors should be aware of it. For more details on these specific risks, please read our blog post on CEFs.

  • Use of high leverage and high fees
  • High volatility (compared to the broader market)
  • Premium over NAVs (market price higher than the NAV)
  • Asset-specific concentration risk.

Concluding Thoughts:

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal yield of less than 1.10%. Many income investors and retirees can't survive on that kind of income. For income investors, we recommend building a core bucket of DGI (Dividend growth) stocks and supplementing it with a basket of CEFs to increase the overall income.

We use our screening process to highlight the best closed-end funds for investment each month. Our filtering process demands that our selections have an above-average long-term performance record, offer an average of roughly 8% distributions, and are relatively cheaper with reasonable discounts. We have also provided a larger list of 20 CEFs from many different asset classes.
