data.title: Lovesac: Tough Market, Ambitious Company (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Lovesac Company (LOVE) reported the company’s fiscal Q4 results from the November-January period on the 26th of March. The furniture company has continued to capture market share through showroom expansion and new product launches, but weak industry conditions continue to weigh on financials. While the industry outlook remains turbulent, Lovesac’s growth ambition and long-term market rebound potential now create significant upside potential in my opinion.

I downgraded my rating on the stock to a Hold rating in my previous December 2024 article, titled “Lovesac: Q3 Wasn't A Disaster, But More Caution Is Needed.” The stock has since lost 49% of its value; meanwhile, the S&P 500 has gained 5%.

Lovesac Q4 Review

The industry backdrop hasn’t been easy on Lovesac. Furniture sales declined by -4.2% across the U.S. market during the company’s fiscal Q4 period, reflecting poor macroeconomic conditions. A mix of weak consumer confidence and low housing sales continues to weigh on consumer demand for furniture, while tariffs cause significant inflation in sourcing costs.

Lovesac’s Q4 results reflect the poor macroeconomic backdrop but also the company’s high ambition to capture market share. Total revenues grew by 2.7% to $248.0 million against a sharp industry-wide sales decline. Lovesac has opened 21 new showrooms during the past year, to a total of 278 after Q4, pushing showroom revenues up by 3.5% to $159.8 million. At the same time, e-commerce demand has improved very well as internet revenues grew by 12.3% to $79.2 million for the quarter. Internet sales channel growth picked up considerably from prior quarters.

A sharp -45.4% decline in other sales channels to $9.0 million didn’t slow down total growth very meaningfully.

Past showroom openings, Lovesac has relaunched the core sectional platform and started to expand its reach from sectionals into other product categories such as the new Snugg seating platform. Such expansion is within Lovesac’s previously outlined strategy of new product platform launches, aimed to drive growth for the brand. Both showroom and product platform expansion continued to drive market share gains in Q4.

Market share gains haven’t come without caveats; Lovesac’s Q4 profitability still clearly reflects issues stemming from poor consumer demand. The gross margin declined by 230 basis points to 58.1%, reflecting margin pressure from tariffs that Lovesac hasn’t been able to transfer fully into consumer prices. Weak consumer demand has also caused intense promotional activity across the industry, pushing the gross margin down further. Even though Lovesac decreased marketing expenses slightly in Q4, total operating expenses grew slightly due to higher payroll costs; growth ultimately comes at a price. Adjusted EBITDA declined by -7.8% year-on-year to $49.6 million.

The focus now shifts to FY2027. Lovesac guides the year’s revenues at $700-750 million, implying positive but moderate 4.0% growth at the midpoint. The guidance range is a clear disappointment compared to Lovesac’s 10-15% long-term target rate that was laid out in the 2024 Investor Day, but slow growth clearly reflects turbulent industry conditions. Lovesac assumes that the market will continue to decline by low single digits, reflecting weak consumer confidence and a slow housing market.

There’s not much visibility to a housing market recovery as mortgage rates are still high after going up considerably in March.

Adjusted EBITDA is guided at $33-44 million for FY2027, up by $2.4 million from FY2026 at the midpoint. Profitability is expected to remain thin with a 5.3% midpoint EBITDA margin as market conditions are still weak; operating leverage from improved sales and stronger pricing, are required to push earnings considerably higher. The gross margin is expected to come in at 56-57%, compared to a considerably higher 59-60% target level.

I believe that the report sets a good overall long-term growth outlook for Lovesac. Industry conditions have remained weak for quite long already without any clear improvements in sight, but Lovesac has regardless managed to report positive growth through market share gains. Assuming a market rebound, or even stabilization, Lovesac should grow at a very good pace. Further, the company is planning to expand past the living room in calendar year 2027, corresponding with Lovesac’s FY2028, creating significant additional market share potential.

The main question seems to be how high Lovesac’s margin potential is in normal industry conditions, as current profitability is clouded by industry headwinds. FY2027 won’t show a major margin rebound yet.

Lovesac’s balance sheet remains strong with $101.9 million in cash and no interest-bearing debts, enabling the company to survive turbulent industry conditions and even repurchase shares opportunistically.

LOVE’s Valuation Is Overly Pessimistic

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Lovesac. I again assume good long-term growth, driven by market share gains and a moderate market rebound through FY2028-FY2030. The FY2026-FY2036 revenue CAGR is now set at 6.6% with 2% perpetual growth after the estimate period.

While the long-term margin level is challenging to estimate, I estimate the EBIT margin to stabilize at 5% in normal industry conditions as a base scenario estimate. Current profitability is clouded by slow industry conditions and growth investments, creating significant expansion potential through future growth.

I assume Lovesac’s cash flow conversion will improve gradually as margins improve and growth slows down. The company’s CapEx is elevated, but Lovesac doesn’t require much working capital to achieve growth.

The estimates put Lovesac’s fair value at $23.9, suggesting a massive 77% upside as of writing. I believe that the industry’s prolonged downturn has made the market overly pessimistic; Lovesac still clearly has great earnings growth potential, currently countered by slowing industry sales. Such upside may be reliant on improved industry sales, and the outlook remains very cloudy, but patience should be rewarded very well.

Lovesac’s 7.9 forward EV/EBITDA is comparable to Ethan Allen Interiors Inc.’s (ETD) 8.9 EV/EBITDA, Bassett Furniture Industries’ (BSET) 8.6 EV/EBITDA, and La-Z-Boy Incorporated’s (LZB) 8.2 EV/EBITDA, but the company should have better growth ahead than the mentioned peers. Further, adjusting away non-interest-bearing capital leases from enterprise value, Lovesac's forward EV/EBITDA stands at only 2.6.

The fair value estimate is down from $29.1 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 11.58%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume Lovesac to draw any debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.43% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.59. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 11.58%.

Takeaway

Even though poor industry conditions continue to weigh on Lovesac, the company is growing through market share gains. New showrooms and expansion into new product platforms and rooms create potential for significantly higher revenues, especially if industry conditions stabilize. The main question regards Lovesac’s margin potential in normal conditions.

LOVE stock’s valuation seems overly pessimistic, and as such, I upgrade my rating back to Buy.

data.title: Rexford: Finding A Good Valuation For Very Little Or No Growth
data.description:
A lot of REITs have gotten into troubled valuations of late due to what I would consider to be issues related to growth problems. Previously, many of these industrial REITs (as well as other sub-sectors of REITs) were trading at very high multiples. Not seldom we're talking about AFFO multiples in excess of 25x AFFO, which analysts argued were part of an impressive growth and fundamental profile. I have always been a bit dubious about such arguments, but it would be wrong to say that my targets have not also been influenced by these trends.

So, it's a healthy exercise to go back to a company like Rexford (REXR), which I managed to "get out of" at the last peak in the late parts of 2025, with a small profit, and see where things should take the company going forward. Why I say this is obviously that over the past few weeks, we've seen a significant decline in the company's valuation, to where Rexford now trades below $32.5/share at the time of writing. While not record lows, it's certainly approaching low levels of valuation.

At this level of valuation, we're talking about a 5.36% yield, which in context is not only high, it's very high for this business. We're also talking about a BBB+ rated REIT with a very attractive debt profile, by which I mean that it's not leveraged to any significant degree compared to other companies. Like other quality REITs, it also manages to hit or beat its targets most of the time - about 90%+ on average over the past 10 years.

There are many arguments for why REXR could perform well.

But what exactly does "well" mean in this context, and why might it not be as great an upside as you might think that it is?

That's what we'll be looking at here.

Rexford Industrial Realty Incorporated - Rebasing cash flow to a low level of growth

The problem with a REIT like this can be stated in a simple illustration.

REITs like Rexford have, unfortunately, been at levels of high valuation in the past. As we can see from previous highs, the company has at times been at 55x P/AFFO, which in hindsight was hardly a justified valuation based on growth, even when that growth for a number of years was double digits. This valuation, which for most of the company's current public "life" has been over 30-35x P/AFFO, has created a situation where a 16-25x P/AFFO looks very undervalued.

But the question is, is it really so undervalued as some investors would have you believe? The current averages for REXR are far more moderate than they once were. Analysts, Wall Street, and Quant ratings are all implying a "HOLD" rating and/or valuation. Quant argues a very high rating in growth—A—that's not a rating I see reflected in the company's current analyst estimates, which are some of the more anemic AFFO growth estimates in REITs I've covered as of late - 1.66% per year going forward until 2028E.

Does the company provide any guidance or material to change these assumptions or estimates, or do the company's filings and numbers provide any such indicators?

Not really, for results. The company's FFO (core) was less than 2%, and the same property NOI (effective) was less than 0.5% for the 4Q25 period, and less than 1.2% for the full year. As such, they further confirm the low rate of growth for the company that analysts estimate.

On the positive side, occupancy is very solid - leasing spreads are good. The company is increasing its dispositions - 7 for the full year at about 12.4% unlevered for $218M. But on the other side, the company is not buying any properties. So, unless the company's proceeds all go towards debt servicing, it's my stance that we're likely to see pressure in AFFO from these dispositions, even if the dispositions themselves can be argued to be made at good levels, and for non-core assets.

The company is doing what I have essentially criticized some self-storage REITs for doing in the past year. They are repurchasing shares using the proceeds from the sales. Why do I not like the company doing this?

The general practice of disposing of cash-generative assets, and, with those proceeds, doing share buybacks, is, to me, except for very low valuation, a very good example of poor capital allocation. The net result of this is reducing the size of the business, reducing revenue, and signaling a lack of investment opportunities, as I have noted in self-storage and other sectors. It may artificially boost company earnings by improving earnings, but it implies prioritization of this above long-term growth.

As a value investor, unless this is done at very specific intervals and situations, I consider this to be poor. In terms of valuation, this practice very much strengthens my assumption that the company needs to be rebased at a conservative valuation. Consider that despite using a quarter of a million dollars to buy back shares, the company only saw 2.6% growth in Core AFFO. Without these buybacks and the 6M shares that the company retired, the growth would have been around $0.03 less based on a share count reduction now included, and the growth would have been around, or less than, 1.5% core AFFO growth. It's my belief that the company's share price would have been even lower.

The company's strong fundamentals and low debt do not change my view on this. Especially if the interest rate were to go up (which I believe is a possibility at this time), given its maturity of about 3 years on average, and many refinancings coming up until 2029E.

The company is even fully expecting a core FFO drop in the coming year. Due to a combination of releasing spread, bad debt, and occupancy decline (which is rare for this business), lower termination income, and unfavorable impacts.

While it would be completely wrong to say that Rexford is having fundamental issues, it would be, in my view, fair to characterize the company as experiencing problematic trends. This is especially true given the characterization that some analysts had 5 years ago, where it was expected that this company would grow double digits until 2030. This has obviously not materialized, nor will it (in my view). The positive thesis continues to be returns at this level, driven by higher share valuations.

I do not believe that the company is likely to see this. The risk-free rate is over 4% now. The company's yield is 5.36%. Without any growth, I find the notion or the argument that there is potential for further returns based on capital appreciation to be fallacious - i.e., why? Why should a company that doesn't grow, or grows very little, be valued at above-market rates? Why should an investor take a risk in a BBB+ investment at 5.3% when we can get a risk-free investment at 4%+ backed by the treasury of the United States of America?

I see no sound argument for this. Based on an average GDF growth multiple, a company (any company) growing at 1.66% should be valued at a multiple of about 11.6x. If we were to use this multiple to calculate a forward fair value based on current AFFO, we'd get significantly negative returns. Now, I am not arguing that REXR is worth only 11x - but I am saying that a rebasing of the company's multiple needs to be done, and it would be irresponsible to work with the historical rates here, because these come at significantly different growth profiles. I have done rebasing of a few REIT and company future earnings and assumptions as of late, and this is now becoming the latest one.

Rexford Valuation - Upside is low, and the potential is less than 10% per year.

If you look at any historical average, the valuation becomes distorted, and the company looks cheap. I argue, as I said above, that it is not cheap. A 10-20% annually growing REXR might have indeed been worth about 20-30x P/AFFO. A 1% growing REXR if you exclude share buybacks, is worth no more than 13-16x P/AFFO - at most.

Assuming the high end of that estimate range, we get that the company would, until 2028E, generate returns of 3.17% per year with the dividend, which means that without the dividend, it would generate negative returns. This may, by some investors, be viewed as an extremely negative outlook for what is, by all considerations, a qualitative business, but what of it?

Realty Income (O) is a qualitative business. It has better credit safety, it has a similar yield, and the company has been trading at about 14-16x P/AFFO for years. Why, you argue, should REXR be any different? Because of California? I view that as a net negative given the economic and migratory implications of the state at this time. No, I argue that much in the same way that O trades at 14-15x P/AFFO, I believe it would be "wrong", in valuation, for REXR to trade any significantly higher than this.

This means that I can at most justify 15x P/AFFO, and for the time being, I'm comfortable forecasting the company at this level.

The rebasing of the company's AFFO, considering the 2025 earnings as well as trends going forward, means I put the company's PT at about $25/share. You may consider this very low, but remember that my PT is put at a level where I want to get a solid upside to a fair value price level - I do not believe I can get that, unless we invest at $25/share.

Other analysts come with targets of about $38 to $46, with an average of $41/share. I'm not sure whether they mean that's where they'd buy, or what the company is worth. Based on the recommendations on these targets, with a substantial majority at "HOLD", I would argue it is the latter - in which case I could mostly agree with the assessment, though I'd cut it lower still.

I view the company's potential to be materially lower than 10% per year, and potentially negative if we exclude yield. Furthermore, if the company divests more assets with capital allocation in the form of buybacks, the business could shrink even further.

Based on this, I see the following thesis.

Thesis

Rexford Industrial is a qualitative, BBB+ rated REIT with a solid yield that is facing very tough times. Combining vacancy rates, very few opportunities, falling market rents, and weaker-than-expected guidance, the company has entered a downward spiral. Despite its very high-quality portfolio, I don't believe the company is worth as much. I do not believe the Inland Empire and SoCal market issues are temporary, but longer-term. I believe there are signs, including falling rents, that the company's core business model of infilling rents is failing in this market.

Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $25/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is not doing. I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $25/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.

data.title: GitLab: Finally Time For A Bullish Stance
data.description:
GitLab (GTLB) has received increased attention among traders and on social media in recent months.

The company delivered a series of what appeared to be a strong series of earnings reports throughout 2025 without causing any meaningful appreciation in the firm's stock price. Then the calendar turned over to this year, and GitLab shares caved in, losing nearly half their value while slumping to new all-time lows and barely hanging onto a $20 price:

For a company that appears to still be performing well from an operational standpoint, it's been incredible to witness this sort of value destruction.

You might wave this away by saying it's been a brutal six-month period for software stocks and SaaS names in particular. That's a reasonable reaction. But even by the standard of software companies, GTLB shares have fallen harder than most, and there's been no sign of a bounce even when the rest of the sector tried to stabilize in early March:

Further pouring salt in the wound, direct GitLab competitor JFrog (FROG) has been one of the better performers in the SaaS space recently, while GTLB stock has been steadily sliding.

GitLab's Recent Earnings

GitLab kept reporting strong earnings over the last year, easily topping expectations, and yet the stock didn't go anywhere. In Guggenheim's Q3 earnings results note, for example, their analyst team wrote that "there seems to be an unshakable disdain for GitLab stock, even if the company exceeds expectations." I couldn't have said it better myself. And it was against this backdrop, where GitLab was barely rewarded for good quarters, that it came into its FY Q4 2026 earnings release, which came out March 3rd.

Once again, the numbers were solid. Revenues of $260 million topped expectations by $8 million and rose 23% year-over-year. Adjusted earnings of 30 cents topped expectations by seven cents.

Despite all the AI-related disruption fears, the company's client base continues to grow. The company reported that it now has 1,456 customers with $100,000+ ARR, a figure that rose 18% y-o-y. The company reported 155 customers with at least $1 million in ARR; that count jumped 26% y-o-y. Furthermore, the company's dollar-based net retention rate was 118%, showing that there is plenty of ongoing demand for GitLab's services.

And, GitLab should have an exciting 2026 ahead of it. As CEO Bill Staples wrote:

"GitLab sits at the heart of how enterprises build and deliver software [...] The launch of the GitLab Duo Agent Platform brings intelligent orchestration to the full software lifecycle, with all of the context needed to unlock step-function gains across every task in software engineering. As code volume explodes, security, compliance, and governance are no longer optional; they're existential. GitLab was built for this environment.”

Clearly, the market doesn't share Mr. Staples' enthusiasm. What might be the source of this discrepancy?

That's likely because GitLab offered its fiscal year 2027 guidance, and it was not as robust as the market had hoped. Specifically, GitLab guided both its quarterly and full-year results to slightly below the prior analyst consensus. Furthermore, with expectations of a 16% revenue increase in fiscal year 2027, that would represent significant deceleration from the company's prior growth rate. The company also offered a lighter adjusted EPS estimate for the year as it is investing more in the business.

Bulls were likely hoping that the company could offset the negative guidance via its share buyback announcement. Clearly, however, this has not worked out as hoped. I'll offer some context here.

GitLab has been steadily diluting itself over the years with its consistent share-based compensation program. The outstanding share count has climbed from about 145 million in 2021 to 170 million today:

With the new $400 million share repurchase program, at a hypothetical average repurchase price of $20 per share, the company could retire 20 million shares of itself. That'd be roughly 12% of the total share count, which is a significant figure. Keep in mind that unless the company greatly throttles back the rate at which it offers SBC to employees, this buyback program is more likely to offset ongoing employee comp rather than meaningfully reduce the share count.

I don't want to totally dismiss the buyback. It's a good sign. However, I don't think it will have a large impact on the company's earnings per share or stock price in the near-term; that will be driven more by the company's operational results.

How GitLab Can Win In The Current Landscape

Intriguingly, after what appears to have been a three-year absence from posting on X, GitLab's CEO returned to the social media platform on Friday. He tweeted out a thread highlighting the company's strategy. His discussion started off as follows:

"AI agents are putting unprecedented load on every major git platform. Multi-tenant SaaS infrastructure is feeling the pressure — ours included. We're hard at work to build-in better performance and scale in prep for the surge of agentic use ahead.

Why is your team's reliability at the mercy of someone else's multi-tenant infrastructure? When agent-driven load spikes, who controls the scaling — you or your vendor?

70% of GitLab's revenue comes from self-managed deployments. We treat it like a first class part of our business."

It's encouraging to see Mr. Staples go out and proactively make the case for GitLab's place in the changing technology landscape. The reference about being "at the mercy" of other companies' multi-tenant infrastructure seems like a direct claim as to what makes GitLab different from its big tech competitors.

On the flip side of the coin, though, one of the persistent bullish narratives around GitLab stock has been that it would be a logical takeover target for one of the big tech shops. If GitLab is going to double down on this particular marketing angle, it could make GitLab a less attractive M&A target. As Staples tweeted:

"GitLab is the only cloud neutral, model neutral DevSecOps platform where you can get started for free and pay only for what you use."

That's a great selling point as long as GitLab is an independent company. But in a hypothetical scenario where Amazon (AMZN) was thinking about acquiring GitLab, this marketing could end up backfiring.

And, on the M&A front, it's also worth mentioning that GitLab has significant exposure to defense and intelligence industry customers, and lists a prominent defense customer as a case study. This could make the company less attractive as an M&A purchase for certain software companies that don't want the hassle of having to deal with national security matters or the political backlash that could result from having inroads in defense contracting.

We also saw significant reductions in spending on certain U.S. government and defense contracts and consulting arrangements in 2025 which could pressure some of GitLab's customer base. Conversely, however, the war in Iran could lead to a significant spending boost for this category and thus potential increased client spend at GitLab.

GTLB Stock Bottom Line

Despite the buy rating in this article, I do not own any shares of GitLab personally yet, though I am getting more tempted as the price keeps dropping. Let me explain my cautiously bullish stance.

GitLab has a decent valuation case here in absolute terms, and especially so compared to other software firms. However, it is swimming upstream in terms of the AI perception. The market is viewing these sorts of companies as victims of AI disruption, and it has proven quite hard to shake that narrative.

GitLab looks quite reasonably priced here considering its growth rate, and that's especially true as compared to the technology sector overall. Yet the company keeps delivering solid earnings, and the share price keeps dropping. The company's recent soft guidance was much more harshly punished than it had been rewarded for past earnings beats.

My personal view is that GitLab is not as threatened by AI developments as the market currently perceives. But I'm not sure what the near-term catalyst is that would cause the AI overhang to finally lift. As long as GitLab posts a decelerating revenue growth rate, it will support the bearish narrative.

My guess would be that GitLab will be a larger and more profitable business three to five years from now. And eventually, the firm's improving fundamentals should translate to a much higher stock price.

I applaud the CEO for taking a more proactive approach in terms of defending GitLab's value proposition and trying to dispel the company's negative perception within the investment community. And the company's share buyback program is a positive as well. If you have a long-term time horizon, this seems like a fine time to open a position in GTLB stock. That said, it's not one of my top picks in software, at least not yet.

data.title: BME: Removal Of Option Writing Can Improve NAV Growth (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

With the ongoing pullback in the market indices, it can be tricky to find a sector that will hold up through 2026. As capital flows out of the technology sector due to the rising threat of AI, I believe that capital will begin to rotate into value stocks. This is where the BlackRock Health Sciences Trust (BME) has the opportunity to shine. When I previously covered BME, I issued a hold rating due to the weak NAV growth and thin dividend coverage at the time. Despite this, BME has outperformed the S&P 500 since my last coverage. The fund has released an updated annual report that also prompted me to revisit the outlook and value proposition going forward.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that BME's share price has declined by about 2.5%. The fund has been able to provide some protection against the market pullback over the last few weeks. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 5.8% over the same time frame. BME now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 8.3% and issues those payouts on a monthly basis.

The fund has also improved its structure since the time of my last coverage. For instance, BME is able to capture a greater level of price growth over time due to the elimination of its option writing strategy. Furthermore, the latest annual report indicates that the underlying NAV has increased over the last year, indicating some improvements in its portfolio efficiency.

Fund Strategy: Who is BME For?

According to the latest fund overview, BME has total net assets of $518.2M that are spread across 164 different positions. The fund has a management fee of 1% of assets, which is high but may be worth it for investors seeking a high yield. As implied by the name of the fund, BME aims to provide attractive total returns through its diverse portfolio of health care positions. The fund puts an emphasis on a high rate of distributions, so the fund is better aligned for investors seeking a high rate of income over time. If you are a younger investor, it would make more sense to focus on maximizing total return and tax efficiency through an ETF, such as the State Street Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV). For instance, we can see that BME underperformed against XLV over the last decade.

Therefore, BME is a great option for retired investors that need to focus on a supplemental stream of income to support lifestyle expenses. The fund will essentially sell off the upside growth of its holdings in order to generate the necessary earnings to support those payouts. So BME is a solid option for investors that don't want to manually sell shares within their portfolio to support lifestyle expenses. At the time of my last coverage, BME implemented an option writing strategy as part of its strategy to generate extra income. However, it seems like BlackRock has removed this feature of the fund, which should allow the positions to capture more upside growth.

When looking at the fund's breakdown by sector, BME is overwhelmingly allocated to pharma, biotech, and life sciences companies at 72.21% of assets. The remaining portion of the portfolio is allocated to health care equipment and services at 26.38% of assets. Therefore, investors should understand that BME exposes investors to concentration risks that can amplify downside movements if the sector faces any specific headwinds.

The fund also provides direct exposure to some of the highest-quality positions across the industry. For instance, the largest individual position is currently Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) at 11.28% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to many other notable positions, such as Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), AbbVie (ABBV), Gilead Sciences (GILD), and Merck (MRK), just to name a few. Since BME removed the option writing strategy, the fund is better aligned to capture the upside growth of these companies.

BME is actively managed, so there is no set rebalancing schedule. Therefore, an investment in BME means that you are putting trust in management's ability to rotate in and out of quality positions. I would argue that this active management style is better than a passive approach because it means that BME can rotate out of any struggling positions quicker compared to a passive index that would be stuck holding a poorly performing position.

Tricky Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, BME traded at a discount to NAV of 8.3%. Despite the recent pullback in its share price over the last few months, the fund now trades at a slightly smaller discount to NAV of 6.06%. For reference, BME traded at an average discount to NAV of 3.46% over the last five year period. Referring to the red line on the graph below, BME continues to trade at one of the largest discounts to NAV valuations over the last ten years. However, I suspect that the elimination of the option writing strategy will help the fund trade back towards the premium territory once the NAV growth improves going forward.

A fund's price-to-NAV valuation is typically a direct reflection of the fund's performance. Funds with a history of eroding their NAV will likely trade at a discount, whereas funds with a history of NAV growth can trade at premiums. With the elimination of the fund's option writing strategy, BME has essentially turned into an equity play at this point. Therefore, I anticipate that the fund will eventually rise back up to the middle of its price-to-NAV range and even has the potential to cross into premium territory if the healthcare sector experiences a rally.

This is supported by management's announcement that they will be implementing tender offers for funds that have a discount to NAV of around 10%. So even if the fund's discount to NAV starts to drift closer to that 10% mark, this would allow management to buy shares, and it may create a floor for the share price. Therefore, this could be a solid time to accumulate shares if you are optimistic about the outlook of healthcare.

I anticipate that lower interest rates can serve as a catalyst for the sector. A lower cost of debt may enable companies to improve their operating spreads, reinitiate growth plans, and fuel acquisitions and can even lead to more capital allocated towards research and development. The challenge is that the Fed decided to leave rates unchanged during their latest meeting, so there's always a risk that interest rates do not trend lower over the next twelve months.

Performance Tradeoffs

Since my last coverage, the fund has released an updated annual report for the 2025 period. The fund reported net investment income of $0.13 per share, while net realized gains landed at $5.79 per share. Looking back over prior years, net investment income levels have always remained a smaller portion of its earnings. Therefore, the fund has always been reliant on net realized gains to fuel its NAV growth and support distributions. The challenge is that this means the fund has to actively sell off the upside growth of its winners over time. Similarly, investors need to trust that management can rotate out of its losing positions before the losses grow too large.

The difficult part is estimating how the fund will perform over time. Since BME is reliant on positive market momentum to capture gains, this means that the fund is basically vulnerable to market declines where it may not be able to capture net realized gains. The end result is a NAV that fluctuates over time and is dependent on positive momentum across the health care sector. For instance, the fund experienced large net realized losses of $2.21 per share in 2022, which it took BME several years to recover from. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $47.96 per share
  • 2022: $43.30 per share
  • 2023: $42.18 per share
  • 2024: $41.20 per share
  • 2025: $43.97 per share

The caveat is that BME's removal of the option strategy means that it will likely be able to capture a greater level of net realized gains going forward, even through milder rallies. The whole dynamic of the fund means that we are outsourcing the ability to sell off gains to BME's management. Furthermore, this means that BME no longer has a way to offset market declines, which can increase reliance on return of capital distributions. Since this is a new dynamic for the fund, we have no idea how BME will hold up against a prolonged decline. Now that the options aren't part of the strategy, BME may see more severe downturns since it will be forced to pay out distributions even if the portfolio didn't generate enough income.

On a more positive note, there are some catalysts for investors to look forward to. For instance, BME has direct exposure to companies like LLY and Novo Nordisk (NVO), which have been able to benefit from the global adoption of GLP-1 pills. For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the GLP-1 weight loss drugs market can increase to ~$48.8B by 2030. This represents a potential growth CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 18.54% over the next few years.

Dividend is Too Generous

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.2621 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.3%. This monthly payout converts to an estimated annual distribution of $3.1452 per share, assuming that the fund keeps payouts consistent over the next twelve months. However, the fund's earnings have only been able to meet this threshold in two of the last five years. For instance, BME's earnings were $5.92 per share in 2025, but the earnings were low at $1.63 per share in the prior year. Therefore, the overall coverage is a bit inconsistent, and several periods of a downward market can threaten the sustainability of those payouts. Therefore, I would remain cautious during times of uncertainty.

Despite my caution, the fund has historically proven to be a great long-term holding for investors that want to compound their annual income. To demonstrate this, I ran a backtest of an initial $100,000 investment at the start of 2017. This backtest assumes that no additional capital was ever invested after the initial investment, but it does assume that all distributions were reinvested back into the accumulation of shares. By simply remaining invested, long-term holders would have seen their annual dividend income more than double.

2017 Dividend Income: $7,805

2025 Dividend Income: $16,765

Lastly, the latest Section 19(a) notice indicates that most of the payouts were supported by net realized long-term gains. The mean is that the fund's removal of an option strategy can improve the tax efficiency of those payouts. Long-term capital gains are classified as qualified income, which has the most favorable tax classification for investors. If BME is able to maintain this payout breakdown, it creates flexibility in terms of what account type BME can be held within.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating of BME to a buy at this time. Following the removal of the option writing strategy, BME has basically transitioned to an equity-focused fund. The portfolio is now directly positioned to capture a greater level of capital appreciation from its positions, and this can lead to healthier NAV growth through favorable market conditions. If the GLP-1 drug market continues to expand, BME's new structure will be able to capitalize on this. The fund still trades at a favorable price to NAV valuation, and although the dividend coverage is a bit too generous, the fund is a proven compounder of dividend income.

data.title: The Market Is Cracking - I'm Getting Ready To Buy My Favorite Stocks
data.description:
Introduction

I just came across an interesting Seeking Alpha news article titled “Investors should capitalize on current market dislocation - strategist.” As I am also a strategist who has advocated buying great stocks on weakness in this market, I obviously clicked on it.

Essentially, it all boiled down to Stephanie Link, who is the chief investment strategist at Hightower, making the case that investors should “capitalize on current market dislocations” instead of waiting for clarity to emerge. That’s basically a way of saying to buy the dip.

And, in general, I think it’s a great point to explain that waiting for clarity is never a good idea. Most of you will be familiar with the famous saying of Warren Buffett:

"Be fearful when others are greedy and greedy when others are fearful."

If you wait until clarity, odds are the market has recovered already. After all, if there’s no obvious risk anymore, why would stock prices be “low” or trade at what some may consider to be obviously undervalued prices?

Stephanie Link said it quite well:

The cost of selling during periods of panic can be substantial, Link emphasized, citing historical examples of dramatic recoveries. Investors who sold during COVID would have missed a 198% recovery, while those who exited during the Silicon Valley Bank crisis in 2023 would have missed a 78% rally.

“If you sold last year during liberation day and that time period, you would have missed a 34% rally from the lows,” she added, noting she is focused on where prices will be one year from now. - Via Seeking Alpha.

The tricky part is that all of these examples saw much steeper S&P 500 (SP500) declines. Right now, the market is down less than 8% from its highs. That’s not even a correction. However, it makes sense that sentiment seems unusually weak, as technology stocks (XLK) are 13% below their 52-week high. Software stocks (IGV) have lost a third of their value. These are all popular investments, so outsized losses in these areas tend to hurt sentiment.

Another issue is what I like to call “what if risk.” That’s a way of saying that the market knows we’re close to a very bearish scenario. That scenario is a prolonged Iran War that keeps oil above $100 for weeks. Such a move could be the final straw for a weak consumer and housing market that results in a recession. Even worse, if structural issues like a closed Strait of Hormuz keep inflation high, we could be looking at the first stagflation since the 1970s. I am not saying this will happen, but this is what’s making people nervous.

Hence, even as the market’s valuation has come down quite a bit (see below), investors know that if this worst-case scenario were to occur, both the “P” and the “E” in the P/E ratio could come down a lot. That would be very bearish.

Bloomberg’s John Authers explained that as well. Without a ceasefire, the U.S. would likely be forced to open the Strait of Hormuz by force. This could take weeks and increase the risks of a big negative economic impact. That’s why global stocks continue to move almost inversely with oil prices, as the cool chart below shows.

As I have said before, I am fully aware of these risks. I’m sometimes naive, but I’m not stupid. There are a number of reasons why I’m bullish.

  • In general, as a long-term investor, I see dips/corrections as opportunities.
  • While reason 1 isn’t enough to build a thesis (the “I’m always bullish” factor is useless in my business), I see structural macro tailwinds.

For Main Street Alpha, which is the business I will bring to Seeking Alpha on April 7 as an Investing Group (more info soon), my business partner, Albert Marko, and I recently looked into the bigger picture with regard to growth and inflation. We wrote that the government’s debt situation and the related need for higher economic growth (the “run it hot” thesis I have often brought up) would likely create a mix of higher inflation and more broad-based economic growth.

[...] the monetary and fiscal authorities are quietly aligned in letting inflation run hot. It is a stealth default. They are inflating away the debt burden while publicly discussing the rising cost of living for obvious political purposes. This creates an environment where running it hot using inflation becomes an unofficial policy.

We are transitioning from a globalist, low-inflation environment, where tech multiples could expand to the moon on the back of zero-interest-rate policy, to a nationalist, high-nominal-growth era. This isn't just a slight sector rotation. It is a full-scale regime shift that forces a radical rethinking of portfolio construction. - Main Street Alpha.

That’s the thesis in a nutshell and the reason why we want to use this opportunity to load up on high-quality value stocks with pricing power, as we believe that’s where alpha is.

So, to go back to the start of this article, the market itself may not reflect a lot of panic, but under the hood, there’s a lot of value to be found. We believe that’s a great way to put capital to work. And, as I have written in prior articles, I may have limited cash right now, as I am preparing to move abroad; I’m looking at investing the most capital “ever” in the months ahead.

That’s what the second part of this article is about.

I See Some Of The Best Opportunities In Years

In this part, I want to show you some of my favorite stocks. I own some of them, others are on my watchlist, and the third group will be placed on my watchlist. Needless to say, in multi-ticker articles, it’s impossible to give each company the attention it deserves. As I usually need more than 2,000 words for an in-depth analysis, I will use this as a foundation for further research in case you’re interested in any of these picks.

One company I like is Carrier Global (CARR), the former United Technologies spinoff that I haven’t covered in years. The last time I covered it was when it was in the process of buying the German Viessmann company, which is a leader in heat pumps and other products.

What I like about them is that they have gone from simply selling HVACs to creating an entire ecosystem. These ecosystems need long-term maintenance and make Carrier a staple in homes and businesses. Also, it caters to data centers. Long-term, it aims for up to 8% annual organic revenue growth, which would be a tremendous number and a great basis for double-digit annual EPS growth.

So far, analysts see an acceleration to 16% EPS growth in 2028. As CARR trades at 20.7x earnings, I think it’s a great buy at current levels, especially if I’m right about growth broadening.

Speaking of growth broadening, I love distribution business models, where companies basically connect sellers of goods (like Carrier) to buyers and offer related services. In this space, I own QXO (QXO), which is an M&A-focused housing distributor. I am very bullish on that one. However, QXO is young. Others, like Watsco (WSO) and Pool Corp. (POOL), are considered the gold standard of the distribution business, as they have spent decades proving their business models.

Watsco is an HVAC distributor. It basically stores HVAC systems and parts that are used for maintenance. Due to housing weakness, it has weakened, yet at a 3.5% yield, I’m starting to really like this player. Also, as I am bullish on housing, I think EPS estimates will improve a lot.

Pool Corp. sells pool chemicals. This makes it cyclical, as new home construction tends to expand its addressable market (more pools). However, there’s also a defensive factor, as every pool owner knows how important (and expensive) it is to maintain a pool.

At a six-year low, I love this business.

In the tech sector (it’s officially a consumer discretionary company), I like Amazon (AMZN). It currently trades at roughly 28x earnings with a growth profile of >20% expected annual EPS growth in 2027 and 2028. What I like about Amazon is that while it is heavily competing in the cloud space, it also has a core business that can adopt AI to grow margins. As it has a somewhat low margin profile due to its e-commerce business, there is likely a lot of fat to trim. Also, unlike most hyperscalers that have their own AI applications, Amazon doesn't compete with Gemini, Cloud, and other systems. I believe the odds of Amazon still being a tech leader a decade from now are way higher than for a software-focused company like Microsoft (MSFT).

Now, to go back to Stephanie Link, she brought up railroads. That’s exactly the theme I like as well, as I love to buy cyclical value on weakness in this market due to my growth acceleration thesis. She brought up Union Pacific (UNP). I completely agree with that pick.

Link is also overweight industrials (XLI), particularly Union Pacific (UNP), where CEO Jim Vena’s operational expertise has impressed her.

She noted that during Vena’s tenure at Canadian Pacific, the stock rose 258%, and she expects similar execution at Union Pacific. The pending Norfolk Southern (NSC) acquisition could deliver $3B in synergies and push earnings power to $17 per share, though Link believes the stock can work even without the deal based on continued economic growth. - Via Seeking Alpha

I am convinced UNP will get approval to buy NSC, as I see substantial strategic tailwinds for the U.S., like a better coast-to-coast connection that can de-risk supply chains in case certain ports cannot be serviced or goods need to flow faster across the nation.

Just like most cyclical stocks I have brought up so far, I am convinced that UNP will see higher growth expectations if my thesis is correct, which should allow it to continue the first long-term uptrend since the end of the prior growth cycle in 2021.

In basic materials, I’m warming up to gold and silver again. However, as I do not invest in physical gold and silver for safety and practical reasons, I prefer streamers, which are companies that finance mining projects. In return, they get to buy a part of the production below spot price, which basically gives them ultra-high-margin gold and silver revenues.

Here, I love Franco-Nevada (FNV), as it also owns oil and gas royalties in the U.S.

Then there’s TransDigm (TDG), which I bought at the end of last year. Currently, I’m down roughly 10% on it. TransDigm sells aftermarket parts for planes. They are a private-equity-like company, as they buy small suppliers on a very frequent basis. Most of these companies sell products with very limited competition. That provides pricing power, as most goods are cheap compared to the total price of a plane.

Right now, however, the stock is down, as the market has started selling everything related to aerospace & defense. It seems that the looming risk of fuel shortages is a factor to discount future growth a lot more. However, I agree with a recent news article on Seeking Alpha about this topic:

Sethi also identified TransDigm (TDG), which operates in the airplane aftermarket, as a compelling opportunity. His reasoning follows a counterintuitive logic: if economic conditions slow new airplane purchases, airlines will need to maintain and repair their existing fleets more frequently. - Via Seeking Alpha

In general, TDG is attractive again, as it trades back at its 10-year average with EPS growth expectations of 18% and 16% in 2027 and 2028, respectively.

Over the past 20 years, TDG has returned nearly 23.0% per year, thanks to strong secular growth, substantial pricing tailwinds, and excellent management in M&A.

It’s unlikely that the growth story is over.

What is over is this article, as it’s now time for the takeaway. However, before I give you my summarizing thoughts, I have to tell you that I will provide a lot more in-depth analysis and actionable ideas. Especially in this market, I think it’s crucial to remain on top of opportunities. And as there are plenty to be found, I’ll dedicate a lot more time to this.

Takeaway

Waiting for clarity never works. That’s what makes investing so tricky. If there’s anything I have learned in my career, it’s that whenever I buy when it feels right, I’m about to make a mistake. Meanwhile, every single one of my best investments was made during times when it felt wrong to buy.

Right now, we’re seeing a lot of weakness under the market’s hood. As I am in the camp that believes we’re closer to a growth acceleration than stagflation, I happily use that opportunity to my advantage. That’s why today was about some of my favorite ideas in this market and a promise to provide a lot more ideas.
